content:
I'm going to try an experiment, where I work through John Baez's topos theory blog posts while "thinking out loud" in this topic. (For discussion of this idea, see #meta: meta > "learning out loud"? ).

A few disclaimers: I'm not sure how far I'll get! Also, my discussion here is unlikely to be self-contained; if you want to follow along, you may need to reference the blog posts. And finally, I am almost certainly going to make a lot of mistakes!

Please feel free to join in, or just to point out when I'm confused about something! I can't guarantee I'll have the energy to give you a full response if you do so, but please know that I will still appreciate whatever you choose to post.

I'm going to take a puzzle/exercise-based approach. I find it helps me focus my thoughts to have a particular thing I'm trying to figure out. (Sometimes I'll even jump straight to an exercise before reading a section! Then that exercise helps motivate my reading.)

The first exercise I want to contemplate is this:

In many of these examples something nice happens. First, suppose we have s∈FUs \in F Us∈FU and an open cover of UUU by open sets UiU_iUi​. Then we can restrict sss to UiU_iUi​ getting something we can call s∣Uis|_{U_i}s∣Ui​​. We can then further restrict this to Ui∩UjU_i \cap U_jUi​∩Uj​. And by the definition of presheaf, we have

(s∣Ui)∣Ui∩Uj=(s∣Uj)∣Ui∩Uj(s|_{U_i})|_{U_i \cap U_j} = (s|_{U_j})|_{U_i \cap U_j}(s∣Ui​​)∣Ui​∩Uj​​=(s∣Uj​​)∣Ui​∩Uj​​

In other words, if we take a guy in FUF UFU and restrict it to a bunch of open sets covering UUU, the resulting guys agree on the overlaps Ui∩UjU_i \cap U_jUi​∩Uj​. Check that this follows from the definition of functor and some other facts!
The exercise is "Check that...", right? Do you have an idea of where to start? :wink:

Morgan Rogers (he/him) said:

The exercise is "Check that...", right? Do you have an idea of where to start? :wink:

Yes, that's right! And I think I do have an idea of where to start! It'll take me a minute to type it out, though.

We have a functor F:O(X)op→SetF:\mathcal{O}(X)^{\mathrm{op}} \to \mathsf{Set}F:O(X)op→Set. Here, O(X)op\mathcal{O}(X)^{\mathrm{op}}O(X)op is the category of open sets of a topological space XXX, where we have a unique morphism from UUU to VVV exactly if V⊆UV \subseteq UV⊆U. I think of a morphism from UUU to VVV as saying "UUU contains VVV".

The first thing I want to note is that O(X)op\mathcal{O}(X)^{\mathrm{op}}O(X)op is a poset. Consequently, all diagrams commute in O(X)op\mathcal{O}(X)^{\mathrm{op}}O(X)op! In particular, this diagram commutes for any Ui,Uj⊆UU_i, U_j \subseteq UUi​,Uj​⊆U:
diagram

Next, I'll use the fact that functors send commutative diagrams to commutative diagrams. That means that this diagram in Set\mathsf{Set}Set also commutes:
diagram

by rU→Vr_{U \to V}rU→V​ I mean the "restriction function" that restricts things from UUU to VVV, for V⊆UV \subseteq UV⊆U. This is the image under FFF of the unique morphism from UUU to VVV in O(X)op\mathcal{O}(X)^{\mathrm{op}}O(X)op.

Now, let's pick some s∈FUs \in FUs∈FU. Since this diagram commutes, we have that rUi→Ui∩Uj∘rU→Ui(s)=rUj→Ui∩Uj∘rU→Uj(s)r_{U_i \to U_i \cap U_j} \circ r_{U \to U_i}(s) = r_{U_j \to U_i \cap U_j} \circ r_{U \to U_j}(s)rUi​→Ui​∩Uj​​∘rU→Ui​​(s)=rUj​→Ui​∩Uj​​∘rU→Uj​​(s). I believe this is just different notation for the thing we wanted to prove!
(side question: how do you make the diagrams (pictures) so fast?)

Peva Blanchard said:

(side question: how do you make the diagrams (pictures) so fast?)

I use this amazing website to draw the diagrams: https://q.uiver.app/ .
Then all I have to do is take screenshots, and paste them into my draft!

Alright, we next have our first official "puzzle"!

Puzzle. Let X=RX = \mathbb{R}X=R and for each open set U⊆RU \subseteq \mathbb{R}U⊆R take FUF UFU to be the set of continuous real-valued functions on UUU. Show that with the usual concept of restriction of functions, FFF is a presheaf and in fact a sheaf.

I'll start by seeking to show that FFF is a presheaf.

To show that FFF is a presheaf, we need to show that it is a functor F:O(R)op→SetF: \mathcal{O}(\mathbb{R})^{\mathrm{op}} \to \mathsf{Set}F:O(R)op→Set. Now, for each open U⊆RU \subseteq \RU⊆R, we have that FUFUFU is the set of continuous-real valued functions on UUU. To talk about "continuous" functions means that there needs to be some topology on UUU. I think it's reasonable to assume that UUU is equipped with the subspace topology it inherits from R\mathbb{R}R.

I'm a bit intrigued by the fact that we then have FU=Top(U,R)FU = \mathsf{Top}(U, \mathbb{R})FU=Top(U,R), where UUU is equipped with the subspace topology. This leads me to consider the functor Top(−,R):Topop→Set\mathsf{Top}(-,\mathbb{R}): \mathsf{Top}^{\mathrm{op}} \to \mathsf{Set}Top(−,R):Topop→Set.

I'd now like dream up some functor G:O(R)op→TopopG: \mathcal{O}(\mathbb{R})^{\mathrm{op}} \to \mathsf{Top}^{\mathrm{op}}G:O(R)op→Topop, so that we can express FFF as F=Top(−,R)∘GF = \mathsf{Top}(-, \mathbb{R}) \circ GF=Top(−,R)∘G.

The first idea that comes to mind is to let GGG act as follows:

  • it sends each open subset of R\mathbb{R}R to that same subset, viewed as a topological space equipped with the subspace topology it inherits from R\mathbb{R}R
  • it sends each morphism U→VU \to VU→V to the morphism from UUU to VVV corresponding to the inclusion function from VVV to UUU
It remains to show that GGG is a really a functor, and that F=Top(−,R)∘GF = \mathsf{Top}(-, \mathbb{R}) \circ GF=Top(−,R)∘G.

I would have gone for a more direct proof that FFF is functor.

spoiler

Regarding the definition of FFF. On object: FFF sends an open subset UUU to the set FUFUFU of continuous real-valued functions. On arrow: FFF maps the containment U⊇VU \supseteq VU⊇V to the function FU→FVFU \rightarrow FVFU→FV that sends a continuous function fff on UUU to its restriction on VVV. At this point, we could dive into the details of why the restriction of a continuous function on a subset of its domain is indeed a continuous function, given the relevant induced topologies on their domains. But that would be an exercise in topology, so ... I'll invoke the "reductio ad obvious-um" ...

The fact that FFF preserve identities and composition amounts to:
* restricting to the entire domain is the same as not restricting anything at all
* when U⊇V⊇WU \supseteq V \supseteq WU⊇V⊇W, restricting a function f:U→Rf : U \rightarrow \mathbb{R}f:U→R on VVV and then restricting on WWW is the same as restricting fff directly on WWW.

I think the crux of the exercise is in proving that FFF also satisfies the sheaf condition.

To show that GGG is a functor, I think the only tricky thing to check (unless I'm missing something) is as follows: We want to show that if UUU and VVV, with V⊆UV \subseteq UV⊆U, are open subsets of R\mathbb{R}R equipped with the subspace topology inherited from R\mathbb{R}R, then the inclusion function i:V→Ui:V \to Ui:V→U is continuous.

To show this, I want to use this property of the subspace topology: "Let YYY be as subspace of XXX and let i:Y→Xi:Y \to Xi:Y→X be the inclusion map. Then for any topological space ZZZ a map f:Z→Yf: Z \to Yf:Z→Y is continuous if and only if the composite map i∘fi \circ fi∘f is continuous".

In our case, we have the inclusion map iV→U:V→Ui_{V \to U}: V \to UiV→U​:V→U and the two inclusions to R\mathbb{R}R, namely: iV:V→Ri_V: V \to \mathbb{R}iV​:V→R and iU:U→Ri_U: U \to \mathbb{R}iU​:U→R. Since iU∘iV→U=iVi_U \circ i_{V \to U} = i_ViU​∘iV→U​=iV​ and iVi_ViV​ is continuous, we conclude that iV→U:V→Ui_{V \to U}: V \to UiV→U​:V→U is also continuous.
@Peva Blanchard I was strongly considering aiming for a more direct proof! I'll be interested to take a look at the "spoiler" in a bit!

It remains to show that F=Top(−,R)∘GF = \mathsf{Top}(-, \mathbb{R}) \circ GF=Top(−,R)∘G. To show this, it suffices to show the following:

  1. Top(−,R)∘G\mathsf{Top}(-, \mathbb{R}) \circ GTop(−,R)∘G sends each open subset UUU of R\mathbb{R}R to the set of real-valued continuous function on UUU
  2. Top(−,R)∘G\mathsf{Top}(-, \mathbb{R}) \circ GTop(−,R)∘G sends each morphism in O(R)op\mathcal{O}(\mathbb{R})^{\mathrm{op}}O(R)op to a function that restricts functions

Let's consider r:U→Vr:U \to Vr:U→V in O(R)op\mathcal{O}(\mathbb{R})^{\mathrm{op}}O(R)op. So, V⊆UV \subseteq UV⊆U. First, we note that Top(−,R)∘G(U)\mathsf{Top}(-, \mathbb{R}) \circ G(U)Top(−,R)∘G(U) first equips UUU with the subspace topology and then gives the set of all real-valued continuous functions on UUU.

Next, we consider Top(−,R)∘G(r)\mathsf{Top}(-, \mathbb{R}) \circ G(r)Top(−,R)∘G(r). G(r)G(r)G(r) is the morphism iopi^{\mathrm{op}}iop from UUU to VVV corresponding to the (continuous) inclusion function i:V→Ui:V \to Ui:V→U. Then, Top(−,R)(iop)=i∗:Top(U,R)→Top(V,R)\mathsf{Top}(-, \mathbb{R})(i^{\mathrm{op}}) = i^*: \mathsf{Top}(U, \mathbb{R}) \to \mathsf{Top}(V, \mathbb{R})Top(−,R)(iop)=i∗:Top(U,R)→Top(V,R). Here, i∗i^*i∗ acts by precomposition, so it sends a continuous function f:U→Rf:U \to \mathbb{R}f:U→R to the function f∘i:V→Rf \circ i:V \to \mathbb{R}f∘i:V→R. We note that Top(−,R)∘G(r)=i∗\mathsf{Top}(-, \mathbb{R}) \circ G(r) = i^*Top(−,R)∘G(r)=i∗ is indeed acting to restrict functions, as desired.

Peva Blanchard said:

I would have gone for a more direct proof that FFF is functor.

spoiler

Regarding the definition of FFF. On object: FFF sends an open subset UUU to the set FUFUFU of continuous real-valued functions. On arrow: FFF maps the containment U⊇VU \supseteq VU⊇V to the function FU→FVFU \rightarrow FVFU→FV that sends a continuous function fff on UUU to its restriction on VVV. At this point, we could dive into the details of why the restriction of a continuous function on a subset of its domain is indeed a continuous function, given the relevant induced topologies on their domains. But that would be an exercise in topology, so ... I'll invoke the "reductio ad obvious-um" ...

The fact that FFF preserve identities and composition amounts to:
* restricting to the entire domain is the same as not restricting anything at all
* when U⊇V⊇WU \supseteq V \supseteq WU⊇V⊇W, restricting a function f:U→Rf : U \rightarrow \mathbb{R}f:U→R on VVV and then restricting on WWW is the same as restricting fff directly on WWW.

I think the crux of the exercise is in proving that FFF also satisfies the sheaf condition.


I've taken a look at this now! Thanks for chiming in! I went for the less direct approach in part because it felt like it made it easier for me to realize that there are topology things going on. (The "reduction ad obvious-um" is great :laughing:, but I wanted to try and work out the details this time!)

I think there's going to be a bit more topology involved in showing that FFF also satisfies the sheaf condition... :upside_down:

:smile: I see, I was not sure which level of details you wanted to work at.

Btw, I find this format very nice. I read John's blog post series on topos not that long ago, but I never took the time to do the puzzles in detail. If you don't mind I'll probably join you on some puzzles, using the "spoiler" feature.

Peva Blanchard said:

:smile: I see, I was not sure which level of details you wanted to work at.

Btw, I find this format very nice. I read John's blog post series on topos not that long ago, but I never took the time to do the puzzles in detail. If you don't mind I'll probably join you on some puzzles, using the "spoiler" feature.

That sounds awesome!
(deleted)

David Egolf said:

I'm going to take a puzzle/exercise-based approach. I find it helps me focus my thoughts to have a particular thing I'm trying to figure out. (Sometimes I'll even jump straight to an exercise before reading a section! Then that exercise helps motivate my reading.)

The first exercise I want to contemplate is this:

In many of these examples something nice happens. First, suppose we have $s \in F U$ and an open cover of $U$ by open sets $U_i$. Then we can restrict $s$ to $U_i$ getting something we can call $s|_{U_i}$. We can then further restrict this to $U_i \cap U_j$. And by the definition of presheaf, we have

$(s|_{U_i})|_{U_i \cap U_j} = (s|_{U_j})|_{U_i \cap U_j}$

In other words, if we take a guy in $F U$ and restrict it to a bunch of open sets covering $U$, the resulting guys agree on the overlaps $U_i \cap U_j$. Check that this follows from the definition of functor and some other facts!

I’ll aim to attempt this exercise today.
